content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Roxanne Diaz, Esq. 
City Attorney 
City of Norwalk 
Richards Watson Gershon 
355 S. Grand Avenue, 40th Floor 
Los Angeles, CA 90071 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-069 
Dear Ms. Diaz: 
March 21, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmember Cheri Kelley 
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. 
QUESTION 
May Councilmember Kelley make, participate in making, and influence decisions to 
approve the installation of an Electric Vehicle Charging Station in the Norwalk Civic Center 
Parking Structure because she owns property within 500 feet from the site? 
CONCLUSION 
The Councilmember may make, participate in making, and influence decisions to approve 
the installation of an Electric Vehicle Charging Station in the Norwalk Civic Center Parking 
Structure because any foreseeable financial effect on the value of the Council member's property 
will only be nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant. 
FACTS 
The City of Norwalk desires to participate in Southern California Edison's .. Charge Ready 
Program," which is a program that provide public agencies with no-cost installation of 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-17-069 
Page No. 2 
infrastructure, metering equipment and parking stall upgrades for electric vehicle charging stations. 
The public agency is responsible for any costs to install payment kiosks, sales tax for use of the 
charging station and on-going electricity meter charges. An easement between the City and 
Southern California Edison is also necessary. The City would charge a user fee for the use of the 
charging station. 
The City's Staff submitted an application and were notified that the City's Civic Center 
Parking Structure would qualify for the program. In order to proceed, City Council authorization is 
required for the formal submittal of an application as well as to approve the funding and authorize 
execution of related documents for the program and to establish a user fee. 
The location of the charging station will be on a small portion of the parking structure. The 
parking structure is used by employees of the State of California and County of Los Angeles as the 
Norwalk Courthouse is adjacent to the Parking Structure, as well as City employees and/or visitors 
to the Civic Center area or the movie theaters across the street from the parking structure. 
Councilmember Kelley owns a property in a condominium complex located at 12873 
Sycamore Village. She rents the Property to a third party and the rental also includes a parking 
space for the renter within the complex. Her property is located 480 feet from the location of the 
charging station. The condominium is buffered from the location of the charging stations by Civic 
Center Drive as well as heavy trees along Civic Center Drive and other buildings in the complex 
including a swimming pool area. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in the making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial 
interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests 
identified in that section. The interest pertinent to the Councilmember that you have asked about is 
in Section 87103(b): "An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect 
interest of $2,000 or more."2 (Section 87103(b).) 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
The Councilmember's interests are not explicitly involved in decisions regarding the 
Project. Therefore, if the financial effect of the decisions at issue can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 
1870l(b).) 
Regulation 18702.2 provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interest in real property is material when the decision: 
2 Your facts may raise other issues that you have not asked about and we do not analyze. For example, the 
tenant is a source of income to the Councilmember. (Section 87103(c).) 


File No. A-17-069 
Page No. 3 
"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of 
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise 
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the 
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial 
interest; 
"( 11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located 
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than 
commercial property containing a business entity where the materiality standards 
are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this prohibition, the 
Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to participate under 
these circumstances if the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to 
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the 
official's property; or 
"( 12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision 
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the 
market value of the official's property." 
Regulation l 8702(b) cautions, however, that "[ n ]otwithstanding Regulations 18702.1 
through 18702.5, the financial effect of a governmental decision is not material if it is nominal, 
inconsequential, or insignificant." 
Based on your facts, we conclude that the foreseeable financial effect on the value of the 
Councilmember's property will only be nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant. As your facts 
indicate, the project itself is a small project in a highly developed area. You stated that you do not 
believe that the project would alter traffic levels or the intensity of use of property surrounding the 
Councilmember's Property, including parking, as the charging station is simply an amenity 
provided to those who already park in the parking structure. You anticipate that there are drivers of 
electric vehicles that already park in the parking structure and this would provide them with the 
ability to charge their electric vehicle while parking. You also stated that notwithstanding the fact 
the Councilmember's property is within 500 feet of the location of the charging stations, given the 
way the condominium is situated within the condominium complex and is buffered from the 
charging station by a four-lane street, heavy landscaped trees and shrubbery and other buildings. 
Based on these facts we conclude the Councilmember does not have a conflict of interest in the 
decision based on her property. 


File No. A-17-069 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
8 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
era) Counsel 
. Wallace 
nt General Counsel

the installation of an Electric Vehicle Charging Station in the Norwalk Civic Center Parking 
Structure because any foreseeable financial effect on the value of the Council member's property 
will only be nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant.
